coredata.dc:title: Green, sustainable, and economical synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for applications in optical displays and light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, V.D.
Vishal, Vishal
Chandan, G.
Bhatia, A.
Chakrabarti, S.
Bera, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increasing attention in recent years because of their multiple benefits, including low toxicity, biocompatibility, simplicity of functionalization, eco-friendly production, and a wide range of applications. However, expensive precursors and manufacture, as well as a time-consuming procedure, limit their economical design, which must be addressed. We provide a straightforward, cost-effective, eco-friendly, and long-term synthesis method for fluorescent CQDs based on an affordable medicinal plant precursor such as Cissus quadrangularis. The nitrogen-doped CQDs produced had a homogenous size distribution (4–9 nm) and green fluorescent nature, as well as strong photo-stability, excellent water solubility, and a quantum yield of roughly 5%. The existence of a large fraction of sp2 and sp3-carbon groups, as well as other hydrophilic oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups, was confirmed. Potential fluorescence processes are also hypothesized. Furthermore, we report a successful demonstration of N-CQDs for possible and cost-effective applications in quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion green emissive light-emitting diodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomaterials
Nitrogen-doped CQDs
Cissus quadrangularis
down conversion LED
Optical display

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Green synthesis of Cissus quadrangularis-derived carbon quantum dots
Fabrication of quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion LEDs
Structural and morphological characteristics of CQDs
Optical and emission properties of CQDs
Proposed fluorescence mechanism of Cissus quadrangularis-derived N-CQDs
Applications of Cissus quadrangularis-derived N-CQDs for quantum dot-based optical displays and LEDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Process flow of a facile and economical green synthesis of carbon quantum dots derived from inexpensive traditional medicinal plant Cissus quadrangularis as a carbon source via hydrothermal technique.

(a)–(b) Representative high-resolution TEM images of N-CQDs. (c) Size histogram of the N-CQDs with curve fitted to the data using a Gaussian model. (d) Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). (e) SAED patterns of the as-synthesized N-CQDs. (f) X-ray diffraction pattern of as-synthesized N-CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs. (b) High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of N-CQDs. (c) High-resolution spectra of N1s. (d) O1s spectra of N-CQDs.
(a) 1H NMR spectra of N-CQDs and (b) 13C NMR spectra of N-CQDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of CQDs. (d) Zeta potential of N-CQDs.
(a) A comparison of UV–vis absorption spectra between CQDs synthesized with or without EDA. Effect of (b) process temperature (c) process time variation on UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs.

(a) A comparison of fluorescence spectra of CQDs synthesized with and without EDA addition. Inset figures show the photographs of both samples under daylight and UV (365 nm, 60 W) light exposure. Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs subjected to hydrothermal (b) process temperature and (c) process time variations. (d) Photographs of as-synthesized CQDs at various process temperatures exposed to daylight and UV light.
(a) FL spectra of N-CQDs excited at different wavelengths. (b)Tyndall effect to explore dispersibility in aqueous solution.
The CIE chromaticity coordinates with CCT according to CIE 1931 (a) and CIE 1976 (b), as determined by excitation-dependent FL spectra of N-CQDs derived from Cissus quadrangularis.

(a) Schematic core–shell structure of synthesized nitrogen-doped CQDs. Proposed absorption and emission mechanisms for Cissus quadrangularis-derived N-CQDs under different illuminations (b) 295 nm UV and (c) 400 nm blue light.
Prototype of casted quantum dot-based optical display patterns illuminated under (a) daylight and (b) to (f) UV light (365 nm, 60W) at various angles.

Prototype demonstration of Cissus quadrangularis-derived N-Cn-coated UV-LED which transformed into a colorful (yellowish-green) light-emitting diode. Fabricated LED under (a) daylight and (b) UV light illumination (365 nm, 60W). (c)–(e) Under forward bias, a converted UV-LED is illuminated alongside an unconverted one.

(a) Emission (electroluminescent) spectra and CIE chromaticity coordinates with CCT according to (b) CIE 1931 and (c) CIE 1976 of the LED fabricated by integrating the UV-LED chip with epoxy resin-N-CQDs phosphor derived from Cissus quadrangularis.
Process dependency of Cissus quadrangularis-derived CQDs.

head.ce:title: Green, sustainable, and economical synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for applications in optical displays and light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to Be University), Mullana. Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana. Ambala (133207), Haryana, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, M M Institute of Medical Sciences and Research, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to Be University), Mullana. Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: M M Institute of Medical Sciences and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, M M Institute of Medical Sciences and Research, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana. Ambala (133207), Haryana, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, DIT University, Dehradun, Uttarakhand, 248001, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of physical sciences, DIT University, Dehradun Uttarakhand (248001), Haryana,India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increasing attention in recent years because of their multiple benefits, including low toxicity, biocompatibility, simplicity of functionalization, eco-friendly production, and a wide range of applications. However, expensive precursors and manufacture, as well as a time-consuming procedure, limit their economical design, which must be addressed. We provide a straightforward, cost-effective, eco-friendly, and long-term synthesis method for fluorescent CQDs based on an affordable medicinal plant precursor such as Cissus quadrangularis. The nitrogen-doped CQDs produced had a homogenous size distribution (4–9 nm) and green fluorescent nature, as well as strong photo-stability, excellent water solubility, and a quantum yield of roughly 5%. The existence of a large fraction of sp2 and sp3-carbon groups, as well as other hydrophilic oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups, was confirmed. Potential fluorescence processes are also hypothesized. Furthermore, we report a successful demonstration of N-CQDs for possible and cost-effective applications in quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion green emissive light-emitting diodes.

ce:section.ce:para: The global need for a more green and sustainable economy has had an influence on various industries, including nanotechnology and materials. Green synthesis of nanomaterials has emerged as a new topic of study in order to create materials and nanomaterials that are reliable, sustainable, and environmentally benign. Nanotechnology can go beyond the constraints of conventional approaches by concentrating on a more sustainable approach, which can help to create a more sustainable and safer future. In this scenario, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), since their unexpected discovery in early 2004, have demonstrated outstanding characteristics such as being environmentally friendly, easy to synthesize, non-blinking, high biocompatibility, electrochemiluminescence, durability, high photostability, extraordinary multi-photon excitation (up or down-conversion) capacity, ease of functionalization, and soluble in water with a high carbon content (up to 99.9%) [1–8]. CQDs and other carbon-based luminous nanomaterials are gaining popularity as viable replacements for organic dyes and heavy metal-based semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (such as CdSe, PbS, and others) due to their diverse applications in bioimaging, biosensing, photonics, optoelectronics, and other fields [9,10].

As a new generation green solid-state light source, light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are widely used in the sectors of lighting, backlighting, and display because of their low energy consumption, high light efficiency, long life, lack of radiation, and other characteristics. LED lighting is the most energy-efficient option available in the world today. When triggered by near-UV, the wavelength down-conversion method to solid-state lighting (SSL) employs down-conversion materials to create visible light. Usually, two types of down-conversion materials are used: phosphors and semiconductor QDs. So far, widely used multicolor LEDs, including white LEDs, have been reported to employ semiconductor QDs such as CdSe/ZnSe QDs, CdTe QDs, CdSe/CdS/ZnS QDs, CuInS QDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs, InP/ZnS QDs, CdSe/ZnS QDs, and so on [11–17]. Even though QD-based phosphors have various advantages in terms of light intensity, color tunability, and energy consumption, the bulk of these QD phosphors includes hazardous elements, such as Cd, Te, Se, and others, which pose environmental concerns. Furthermore, these phosphors are produced using rather expensive precursors at high reaction temperatures, limiting their wider application in optoelectronics. Down-conversion multicolor or white LEDs based on CQDs are a very good option in this situation. Several organic and inorganic precursor-derived CQDs for down-conversion multicolor or white LEDs have been demonstrated in the literature [18–27].

However, designing and manufacturing a simple, cost-effective, large-scale, eco-friendly process that uses low-cost precursors is critical. Natural biomaterial resources are typically preferred over other organic, inorganic, or synthetic resources in the search for an efficient, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly synthesis method of CQDs in this scenario because they are renewable and biocompatible and can convert biomass waste into worthwhile and valuable materials [28]. Natural precursors might likewise be used as a chemical substitute. Natural precursors also outperform chemicals in the synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials for a variety of reasons, including low cost, nontoxicity, and availability. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the down-conversion LEDs derived from biomaterials.

So far, many synthetic techniques for CODs have been reported, including top-down approaches (arc-discharge, laser ablation, and electrochemical oxidation, among others) and bottom-up approaches (microwave, ultrasonic, solvothermal, and hydrothermal treatment, among others) [28]. Green synthesis processes, on the other hand, are far more acceptable than physical and chemical procedures. Because of its low cost and ease of application, hydrothermal synthesis is the most commonly used green synthesis approach in this area. Plant leaves, petals, and roots are examples of biomaterials utilized as carbon sources for the green synthesis of CQDs [29–32] Aside from these, various additional natural resources are being actively researched, including shells, kernels, bark, stem, fruit juice, and peels [33–38]. Medicinal plants/herbs, on the other hand, are receiving more attention as a forerunner. Cissus quadrangularis is a traditional medicinal plant native to Asian countries that belongs to the “Vitaceae” family and is endowed with immense medicinal properties for thousands of years. Historically, it has been used to treat a variety of illnesses, such as hemorrhoids, gout, asthma, and allergies.

This research aims to develop an eco-friendly, sustainable, and cost-effective green synthetic route for the synthesis of CQDs from low-cost and nontoxic natural resources such as the medicinal plant Cissus quadrangularis for potential applications in QD-based optical displays and down-conversion LEDs.

Cissus quadrangularis leaves and stems were obtained from the university's herbal garden and surrounding locations. All of the chemicals utilized in this study were of analytical grade. All chemicals were utilized just as they were supplied, with no additional purification. Other materials purchased from a commercial supplier included Whatman filter paper (grade 1), 0.22 μm syringe filter (polytetrafluoroethylene membrane), acetone, ethanol, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA), and isopropyl alcohol. A Milli-Q system was used to obtain deionized water (18.2 MΩ/cm, Millipore, France).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of Cissus quadrangularis-derived carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: An inexpensive and facile one-step green synthesis route based on hydrothermal was used to synthesize CQDs from the leaf and stem extracts of Cissus quadrangularis. To get the juice, the stem and leaf portions were sliced into small pieces and mashed in a domestic mixer grinder. Following that, a 3:7 (v/v) ratio of 99.9% pure ethylenediamine (EDA) solution was added with the fresh juice of Cissus quadrangularis plant extract. The solution mixture was then ultrasonicated for roughly 10 min before being put in a 50 ml stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon. This hydrothermal process was repeated for 1–12 h at temperatures ranging from 150 to 220 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the as-synthesized solution produced was centrifuged for 15 min at 10000 rpm. After centrifugation, the impurities settled, and the supernatant solution was collected. To remove any leftover pollutants not removed by centrifugation, the supernatant solution produced was filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe filter (polytetrafluoroethylene membrane). After that, the obtained purified solution was freeze-dried in lyophilizer under −40 °C and kept for later use. The pH value of the as-synthesized CQDs was measured to be around 10.70. The complete procedure is depicted graphically in Fig. 1 .

The ultra-centrifugation of the sample was done with either REMI R8C or CPR-30 Plus centrifuge. The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Molecular Devices SpectraMax iD3 spectrophotometer with a step of 1 nm in the wavelength ranges 230–700 nm and 250–700 nm, respectively. The stirring of the sample was done using a REMI MS-500 magnetic stirrer. The nature of surface-functionalized CODs was assessed using a Shimadzu IRSpirit attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transforms infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectrophotometer in the wavelength range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectra were measured by Physical Electronics PHI 5000 Versa Probe III ranging between 0 and 1200 eV. The crystalline nature of the CQDs was determined using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer at 40 kV, 40 mA and a nonmonochromatic Cu K
α X-ray with an angular range (2θ) of 5–90° and an angular step of 0.02°. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic (13C-NMR and 1H-NMR) measurements were carried out on a 500 MHz Bruker Advance Neo NMR spectrometer. Surface Zeta potential was measured by using Anton Paar Litesizer 500. The morphologies and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of CQDs were investigated using a JEOL JEM 2100 Plus high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The JEOL JEM-2100F's Energy-Dispersive x-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) was used to evaluate the relative concentration of carbon and oxygen in as-synthesized CQDs.

The quantum yield (QY) of the produced CQDs was estimated using the following formula: Φ = Φr (Ix| Ir)(ηx 2|ηr 2)(Ar |Ax), where x denotes the CQDs, r is used for the standard dye (quinine sulfate dye), I is related to the fluorescence emission intensity of the respective system, A refers to the optical absorption values, ɳ denotes the refractive index value of the material. The QY of CQDs was found to be around 5%. Indeed, the higher quantum yield of CQDs obtained from various parts of medicinal/herbal plants remains a source of concern, primarily because plant extract contains a medicinally diverse mixture of many plant metabolites, such as alkaloids, minerals, vitamins, glycosides, phenolics, terpenoids, and flavonoids, and even after careful processing, improving QY remains difficult when compared to simpler inorganic or organic resources. In this regard, supplemental Table S1 compares the quantum yield of CQDs synthesized from diverse medicinal plants/herbs using data from the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using commercially available epoxy resin, a prototype of a QD-based optical display device was developed. Epoxy resin and hardener in various volume ratios, such as 1:1, 1:2, 2:2, and 2:1, were weighed and well mixed with the concentrated CQD solution (1μg/1 μl) before being put in an ultrasonic bath for 10–15 min. The solutions obtained were then poured into stencils to cast the alphabets and the casted alphabets when exposed to ultraviolet radiation, the solution with epoxy resin and hardener with volume ratio of 2:1 worked well as the casted alphabets with this solution gave the most intense green fluorescence under ultraviolet radiations. After optimizing the ratio, the solution was used into stencils to cast the desired design for display (in this case alphabets were casted). After that, the casting design was cured for 24 h at ambient temperature. When exposed to ultraviolet (UV) radiation (365 nm, 60W), the casting pattern glows a greenish hue. Stock colloidal solutions of CQDs in Eppendorf were sealed in clear plastic bags to protect them from ambient moisture in order to increase their stability and simplicity of usage in device applications. For the fabrication of CQD-epoxy resin phosphor, epoxy resin and hardener were mixed in a vial with 2:1 ratio and stirred constantly for about 5 min, until the constituents were well blended and no air bubbles were visible. To this mixture, a concentrated CQD solution (1μg/1 μl) was added and the mixture was well stirred in an ultrasonic bath for around 5–10 min. The mixture was then transferred to a prefabricated hard paper mold containing an LED and allowed to rest at room temperature for 48 h. The hard paper mold was removed after 48 h, and the fabricated LED was ready for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characteristics of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characteristics of the as-synthesized fluorescent N-CQDs were investigated by HR-TEM analysis. The TEM image and corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of the as-synthesized N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 a–d. The TEM micrographs (Fig. 2a and b) shows that the as-synthesized materials are small, monodispersed, and spherical in shape. CQD sizes are largely distributed in the range of 4–9 nm, with an average diameter of 5.1 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 2a-c, indicating the nanoscale of CQDs for their quantum-confinement effect. The elemental composition of the as-synthesized N-CQDs, on the other hand, was investigated using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The presence of high carbon (wt. percent 48.37), nitrogen (wt. percent 10.39), and oxygen (wt. percent 41.24) concentrations in energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy observations suggests the presence of various functionalization groups (such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, and carboxyl groups) on the surface of produced N-CQDs. The oxygen groups present in the functional groups are most likely responsible for the greater proportion of oxygen in the N-CQDs' EDX spectrum. Furthermore, the broad diffused ring in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern demonstrated the poor poly-crystalline structure of the as-synthesized N-CQDs (Fig. 2e).

The crystallinity of the as-synthesized N-CQDs was confirmed by XRD as shown in Fig. 2f. The acquired broad diffraction peak with a center angle of 23.8 corresponds to the (002) plane, which was ascribed to the interlayer packing of disordered carbon atoms in the hexagonal graphite structure of CQDs, resulting in the production of a turbostratic structure [39–42]. Furthermore, the broad peak was caused by the amorphous nature of the formed particles. The presence of a weak peak at 42.1° shows a degree of graphitization along the (100) plane. The interlayer distance (d-spacing) was determined to be 0.37 nm for the (002) plane and 0.21 nm for the (100) plane.

The nature of nitrogen functional forms contained in as-synthesized N-CQDs was investigated further using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a) displays a prominent graphitic C1s peak at around 284.5 eV, a clear N1s peak at 398.8 eV, and an O1s peak at 529.9 eV. The C1s signal was resolved into three components, each centered at 282.7 eV, 283.9 eV, and 285.9 eV, representing sp2C-sp2C, N-sp2C, and C–OH bonds (see Fig. 3b) [43–45]. Apart from the well-known C–N bond obtained at (283.9 eV), the high-resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. 3c) may be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks, each centered on 397.2 eV and 397.7 eV and 398.8 eV resulting from pyrrolic/pyridinic-N, quaternary-N (N–C3), and O=C–N, demonstrating that nitrogen atoms were effectively doped in CQDs [43,44]. Furthermore, the O1s spectra exhibited in Fig. 3d can be resolved into two components centered at 528.8 eV and 529.9 eV, indicating the existence of the C= O and C–OH/C–O–C groups [46,47]. The atomic percentages of C, N, and O were calculated to be around 59.66, 8.14, and 32.2, respectively, which is consistent with the values obtained from EDX spectra. The XPS results thus show that the interaction between the extract of Cissus quadrangularis and ethylenediamine functionalized the surface of the as-synthesized CQDs with various oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups.

Meanwhile, NMR spectroscopy was used to inspect the sp3 and sp2 composition of the core. The 1H and 13C NMR of CQDs was used to discriminate between protons and carbons of sp3-hybridized carbon atoms and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. The 1H NMR spectra as displayed in Fig. 4 a demonstrate the presence of four different chemical shift (δ) regions: 1–3 ppm (dominated by sp3 C–H protons), 3–6 ppm (associated with protons of hydroxyl, ether, and carbonyl groups), 6–8 ppm (for aromatic or sp2 protons), and 8–10 ppm (related to aldehydic protons) [48]. Fig. 4a reveals that the first two areas stated above prevail over the final two regions, showing that sp3-hybridized carbon atoms dominate over sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. CQDs have been shown to be combined with sp3 and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 4b, the 13C NMR spectra indicated a combination of sp3 and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. There were four unique zones identified, namely 20–80 ppm (for sp3 carbons and carbons linked with hydroxyl and amine groups), 80–100 ppm (for carbons attached with ether linkages), 100–120 ppm (for C=C aromatic or sp2-hybridized carbon atoms), and 175–190 ppm (for C=O carbons).

FTIR data revealed the presence of various nitrogen and oxygen functional groups and linkages in CQDs as shown in Fig. 4c. The presence of –OH, –C–H, C=O, N–H, and C–N was clearly indicated by stretching frequencies at 3334.39, 2952.51, 1624.45, 1356.56, and 923.37 cm−1, respectively [49]. Because of the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups, the CQDs that are produced are exceedingly water soluble.

To obtain the surface charge, the zeta potential of CQDs, also known as the electrokinetic potential of the synthesized fluorescent CQDs, was measured using a zetasizer. This technique also sheds light on the properties of CQDs, such as double-layer attributes with a variety of hydrophilic or hydrophobic functional groups. The value of zeta potential is determined by the stability of CQDs particles over short or long-term stability. As shown in Fig. 4d, a zeta potential of −7.8 mV was measured, indicating that CQDs are relatively stable in an aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and emission properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical absorption peak of the as-synthesized CQDs without the addition of EDA was discovered in the UV region, as shown in Fig. 5 a, with a maximum absorption commencing about 284.8 nm and a tail continuing into the range of 366.1 nm. This is due to the typical n–π∗transitions in the graphene-like core and the π–π∗ transition of the conjugated C=C band [50]. However, as shown in Fig. 5a, the maximum absorption peak was found to be displaced to 380.7 nm, whereas the second absorption peak was seen at 476.6 nm for the EDA added as-synthesized CQDs. The presence of functional groups on the core's edge is thought to be responsible for the formation of the second absorption peak. This, in turn, will help in the transfer of negatively charged ions from the non-bonding orbital of the heteroatom to the π∗ orbital of the core, leading in n–π∗ transitions in prepared CQDs. Furthermore, these absorption transitions between the core and edge states generate hole-electron excitons in as-synthesized CQDs in aqueous environments.

Following that, the effect of process conditions such as temperature and time on CQDs synthesized with EDA was investigated further. Both absorption peaks were seen to be pushed toward higher wavelengths, 436.3 nm for 150 °C process temperature and 459.4 nm for 220 °C process temperature, but not noteworthy for the other two process temperatures, 180 °C vs 200 °C. (see Fig. 5b). A similar tendency was also seen for process time variation while maintaining the process temperature constant, as shown in Fig. 5c. Extra functional groups were produced during process variation, particularly at higher temperature and longer duration interactions between EDA and precursor, are thought to be responsible for the significant change in absorption peaks. As a consequence, based on consistency and repeatability, as well as UV–Vis and fluorescence characteristics, the hydrothermal process parameters were optimized to be 180 °C and 3 h. Furthermore, the standard Tauc plot was employed to determine the fluctuation in the optical energy band gap of the synthesized CQDs as hydrothermal process temperatures varied between 150 and 220 °C [50]. The estimated optical energy band gap was determined to be in the range of 2.4–3.06 eV, as indicated in Table 1 and supplementary data (Fig. S1 and Table S2).

CQDs, on the other hand, are characterized by their emission wavelength and size-dependent fluorescence activity. The fluorescence (FL) spectra of CQDs are seen in Fig. 6 a. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence emission from CQDs synthesized with EDA was about 1.8 times stronger than that from CQDs synthesized without EDA at the same concentration (1 mg/ml) of CQDs dispersed in aqueous solutions (DI water). These findings suggest that nitrogen doping contributes to the improvement of fluorescence emission capabilities. Furthermore, the emission peak for N-CQDs produced with EDA addition fluctuated from 320 to 610 nm following stimulation with a wavelength of 260 nm. The deconvoluted fluorescence spectrum revealed three separate wide peaks, with maximum emission peaks occurring at 386, 474, and 515 nm, respectively, with percentage contributions of 18.72%, 44.94%, and 36.32%, suggesting the dominance of greenish-color fluorescent emission (Fig. 6a). In daylight, the aqueous solutions of both CQDs were pale yellow and clear, but with UV irradiation (365 nm, 60 W), they exhibited bright and intense green fluorescence, as seen in the inset of Fig. 6a. Meanwhile, the effect of process factors such as temperature and time on fluorescence emission from CQDs with EDA was examined. It was discovered that as the process temperature increased, the emission spectra broadened, but the maximal emission peaks remained virtually the same at a wavelength of 480 nm, with the exception of the highest peak shifting to 390 nm for the 220 °C treated sample (see Fig. 6b). Fig. 6c shows the photographs of the aqueous solution of N-CQDs synthesized at various temperatures under visible light and a UV lamp of 365 nm, respectively. However, no substantial shift in peak maxima was found when the processing time varied at a certain process temperature (180 °C), as shown in Fig. 6d. Table 1 summarizes the process-dependent fluorescence emission. The fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 7 a demonstrates that the fluorescence emission wavelength of CQDs peaks at 491 nm with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. When the excitation wavelength was raised from 250 to 445 nm, the emission wavelength moved from 348 to 527 nm. As the excitation wavelength rises, the fluorescence emission peaks shift toward redshift, suggesting photon reabsorption. The results indicate that CQDs have excitation-dependent fluorescence, which is related to the quantum-confinement effect and edge defects. The aqueous solution of N-doped CQDs showed a long-term homogeneous phase with no visible precipitation at room temperature. We utilized a red laser beam to illuminate the aqueous solution and clearly witness the Tyndall effect to explore dispersibility in water (as shown in Fig. 7b). These findings demonstrated that the synthesized N-CQDs have exceptional fluorescence characteristics and water dispersibility.

The excitation-dependent FL spectra, on the other hand, might be valuable in designing and implementing the down-conversion approach for colored LEDs. As a result, Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates, color rendering index (CRI), and correlated color temperature (CCT) have been computed in accordance with CIE 1931 and CIE 1976, as shown in Fig. 8 a and b, respectively [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed fluorescence mechanism of Cissus quadrangularis-derived N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs exhibit adjustable photoluminescence and excitation-wavelength-dependent emission. The color of fluorescence is commonly thought to be caused by electronic bandgap transitions of conjugated π-domains, surface defect states, local fluorophores, and element doping (with nitrogen, sulfur, other heteroatoms, and metal ions). Based on the observed XPS, FTIR, and fluorescence spectra of Cissus quadrangularis-derived N-CQDs, a scheme of energy band structure of CQDs has been presented in this work, as shown in Fig. 9 . In lower energy trap sites, the observed excitation-dependent FL emission shows significant diversity amongst particles. These fingerprint emissions clearly show that it is made up of a variety of chromophoric band-gap structures created by groups related to C and N elements, such as C–C/C=C (intrinsic-C), C–N (graphitic-N), N-containing heterocycles (pyridine N), and C=O groups or grain boundaries [52]. A surface defect is a border zone or spherical shell that is separate from the carbon core region or body. The spheroidal area comprises a number of chemical groups formed from sp2 and sp3-hybrid carbons, as well as additional surface functional groups and dangling bonds. Surface defects are primarily caused by surface oxidation and can function as a capture site for excitons, resulting in surface defect state fluorescence. Under UV (295 nm) and visible blue light (400 nm) illuminations, the optical band-gap structure of N-CQDs synthesized from Cissus quadrangularis is postulated, as illustrated in Fig. 9a and b, respectively. Under 295 nm UV excitation, the absorption of UV photons by the localized π -electron in double bonds (mostly C=C or C=N) produces an electron-hole pair (i.e., exciton) during the electron transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of carbon core, H3 → L3. This exciton may create UV light via radiative recombination after vibration relaxation along route-I (L3 →H3). The excited electron, on the other hand, may experience an inter-band transition from a higher LUMO to a lower LUMO through the routes L3 →L2 or L3 →L1, following which it produces visible light by radiative recombination via routes II (L2 →H2) or III (L1 →H1). Meanwhile, when exposed to 400 nm visual stimulation, conjugated π-electrons elevate to H2 →L2 after absorbing blue photons and are capable of imparting excitons and creating visible light through path-I' (see Fig. 9b). It may also take the route-II’ (L1 →H1) during vibration relaxation. As seen in Fig. 9b, such an excitation yields photons with emission wavelengths that are approximately 490 nm longer than the absorbed wavelengths (400 nm). Therefore, according to the findings of the XPS investigations, the nitrogen forms identified on CQDs may be classified into four types: amino, pyrrole, pyridine, and graphite. The amino N is scattered around the surface of CQDs, while the pyrrole N and pyridine N can be found at the structure's edge and core, respectively, and graphite N is doped on the CQDs' core. CQDs' fluorescence capabilities are further demonstrated by their obvious nitrogen structure and dispersion. It is believed that amino groups can boost the fluorescence of CQDs by self-passivation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of Cissus quadrangularis-derived N-CQDs for quantum dot-based optical displays and LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: As previously detailed in the experimental part, a prototype of a QD-based optical display was fabricated using commercially available epoxy resin. The casting pattern glows a greenish color when subjected to ultraviolet (UV) radiation (365 nm, 60W), as seen in Fig. 10 (a)–(f). The emission of CQDs, like that of other nanoparticles, tends to self-quench in solid state due to aggregation. To avoid this unwanted impact, the surface of CQDs must be passivated to immobilize/separate nanoparticles while retaining their emissive capabilities. The use of these matrices not only retains the optical properties of CQDs, but also leads to composite materials that are promising for optoelectronic devices. CQDs often are embedded in a variety of organic and inorganic matrices, including polymers, epoxy resin, starch, silica, and others, to accomplish this purpose [53]. In this study, epoxy resin was utilized to make down-conversion LEDs, the details of which are described in the experiment section. Fig. 11 a-e show the emission of a greenish color from the converted UV-LED when bias is applied. The quality of light generated, as well as the efficiency parameters of the fabricated LED, were also investigated, as shown in Fig. 12 . The electroluminescent (EL) spectrum of the fabricated LED at a forward bias current of 15 mA is shown in Fig. 12a. As shown in Fig. 12b and c, the yellowish-green light emitted by the CQD-epoxy phosphor has Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) chromatic coordinates of (0.41, 0.49) according to CIE-1931 and (0.20, 0.55) according to CIE-1976. The obtained CCT is 3965 K, with a Duv of 0.0378. The current demonstration successfully illustrates that the N-CQD-epoxy composite may be utilized to make yellowish-green LEDs by simply converting the UV-LED chip. However, further study might concentrate on the critical optical properties (such as CIE and efficiency) of CQD-epoxy-based down-conversion LEDs.

To summarize, we present a simple, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly method for producing green fluorescence emitting CQDs from a low-cost traditional medicinal plant extract, Cissus quadrangularis. Because of efficient encapsulation by epoxy resin, the as-synthesized nitrogen-doped CQDs not only retain good fluorescence characteristics in aqueous solutions but also exhibit strong fluorescence in the solid form. The nitrogen-doped CQDs had a homogenous size distribution (4–9 nm), were green fluorescent, had high photo-stability, and had a quantum yield of about 5%. NMR, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, on the other hand, verified the existence of sp2 and sp3-carbon groups, as well as numerous hydrophilic oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups. Potential fluorescence processes are also hypothesized. Furthermore, CQDs are commonly produced from organic or inorganic precursors for optoelectronic applications; however, there has been no report on derivation from natural biomaterials. As a result, we show here the successful development of N-CQDs produced from Cissus quadrangularis for prospective and low-cost applications in QD-based displays and down-conversion LEDs. The commercial UV LED was modified to become a yellowish-green LED. Finally, we anticipate that a low-cost, green synthesis of CQDs from natural biomaterials will expedite the development of low-cost, large-scale QD-based optical displays and LEDs over the next few decades.

Varun Dutt Sharma: conceptualization, methodology, investigation, formal analysis, writing- original draft preparation. Vishal: methodology, investigation, formal analysis. G. Chandan: investigation, formal analysis. Anita Bhatia: visualization, investigation. S. Chakrabarti: methodology, supervision. M. K. Bera: conceptualization, methodology, resources, writing- reviewing and editing, overall supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtsust.2022.100184.

J. Colloid Interface Sci.
